coredata.dc:title: Glucose detection via glucose-induced disaggregation of ammonium-modified tetraphenylethylene from polyanion
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Huihui
Zhu, Ling
Zhu, Xinyue
Zhang, Haixia
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent glucose probe designed by a new strategy was reported. The probe consisted of ammonium-modified tetraphenylethylene (TPEA) with the feature of aggregation-induced emission and phenylboronic acid-containing polyanion that induced aggregation of TPEA via electrostatic interaction. With adding glucose and glucose oxidase, the produced H2O2 converted phenylboronic acid into weaker acid phenol, which reduced negatively charges on the polyanion, resulting in the disaggregation of TPEA. This probe well qualified glucose in the range from 4 to 50μM with detection limit of 2.6μM. Moreover, it exhibited excellent selectivity and was successfully applied to blood glucose detection. As far as we know, no report has been published on glucose detection based on acidity difference between phenylboronic acid and phenol.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Glucose
Boronic acid
Polyanion
Tetraphenylethylene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: BAP-induced aggregation of TPEA for glucose detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Working principle for glucose detection via TPEA-BAP.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of TPEA after adding different concentrations of BAP. (B) Fluorescence spectra of TPEA-BAP before and after addition of H2O2 (20μM). (C) Fluorescence spectra of TPEA-BAP before and after addition of glucose (500μM) and GOx (1UmL−1). Experimental conditions: TPEA concentration: 1μM, BAP concentration: 1μM, solvent: 10mM PBS buffer, pH: 7.4, incubation time: 2h, temperature: 25°C.

(A) Effect of incubation time on glucose detection, pH: 7.4. (B) Effect of pH on glucose detection, incubation time: 50min. Experimental conditions: TPEA concentration: 1μM, BAP concentration: 1μM, GOx concentration: 1UmL−1, glucose concentration: 500μM, solvent: 10mM PBS buffer, temperature: 25°C.

Enzymatic detection of glucose (50μM) via TPEA-BAP in the presence of other sugars (50μM) (A) and various interfering substances existing in serum (B). Black bar represents the addition of corresponding interference and red bar represents the subsequent addition of glucose. The concentrations of amino acids in B (from left to right) are 0.6, 2.8, 2.7, 1.1, 1.6, 2, 0.8, 1.7, 0.6, 0.1, 1.2, 3, 0.8, 0.5, 0.84, 4, 0.16μM, respectively. The concentrations of ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid and glutathione in B are 1μM, 0.03nM, 3μM and 4μM, respectively. The concentrations of interfering substances in B correspond to 100-fold dilution of their concentrations in human serum. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of TPEA-BAP after adding different concentrations of glucose (0, 4, 6, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100, 200, 300, 500μM) in the presence of GOx. (B) Linear correlation between decreased fluorescence intensity and glucose concentration (4–50μM).
Comparison between TPEA-BAP and reported phenylboronic acid-based fluorescent glucose probes.

row.entry: Graphene QDs a and BBV b

head.ce:title: Glucose detection via glucose-induced disaggregation of ammonium-modified tetraphenylethylene from polyanion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Lanzhou Petrochemical Corporation, Lanzhou 730060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Lanzhou Petrochemical Corporation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent glucose probe designed by a new strategy was reported. The probe consisted of ammonium-modified tetraphenylethylene (TPEA) with the feature of aggregation-induced emission and phenylboronic acid-containing polyanion that induced aggregation of TPEA via electrostatic interaction. With adding glucose and glucose oxidase, the produced H2O2 converted phenylboronic acid into weaker acid phenol, which reduced negatively charges on the polyanion, resulting in the disaggregation of TPEA. This probe well qualified glucose in the range from 4 to 50μM with detection limit of 2.6μM. Moreover, it exhibited excellent selectivity and was successfully applied to blood glucose detection. As far as we know, no report has been published on glucose detection based on acidity difference between phenylboronic acid and phenol.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose detection has long been of great interest due to its important role in the diagnosis of diabetes mellitus, which is a common metabolic disease and can cause many serious complications including blindness, high blood pressure, heart disease and kidney failure [1]. Up until now, strategies for glucose detection include electrochemical [2–4], colorimetric [5–7] and fluorescent methods [8–10], among which fluorescent method is considered universally as the most effective and convenient tool due to its distinct advantages of high sensitivity, specificity, and rapidness [10].

Phenylboronic acids have been widely used as the receptors of fluorescent glucose probes [11–16], which can be divided into two categories. One category is nonenzymatic system [11–13], which relies on that phenylboronic acid reacts with glucose to from five-membered cyclic ester. The other category is enzymatic system [14–16], which relies on that phenylboronic acid is oxidized by H2O2, produced in glucose oxidation catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx), to form a phenol. Enzymatic system exhibits excellent selectivity, as GOx catalyzes glucose oxidation specifically.

Tetraphenylethylene is a fluorophore with the feature of aggregation-induced emission, which is non-emissive when molecularly dispersed in solution but becomes highly emissive when aggregated under external stimuli because of the restriction of intramolecular rotation that prohibits energy dissipation via non-radiative channels [17–19]. Recently, several fluorescent nucleic acid probes based on positively charged tetraphenylethylene derivatives were reported [20–22]. The principle was that nucleic acid as a polyanion induced aggregation of positively charged tetraphenylethylene derivative via electrostatic interaction.

Inspired by these fluorescent nucleic acid probes, we designed a fluorescent glucose probe (TPEA-BAP), consisting of ammonium-modified tetraphenylethylene (TPEA) and phenylboronic acid-containing polymer (BAP). Response mechanism is illuminated in Fig. 1 . TPEA was soluble and molecularly dispersed in aqueous solution. The dissociation of phenylboronic acid converted BAP into a polyanion, which induced aggregation of TPEA via electrostatic interaction. With adding glucose and GOx, phenylboronic acid reacted with the produced H2O2 to form weaker acid phenol, which reduced negatively charges on BAP, resulting in the disaggregation of TPEA.

4-Hydroxybenzophenone, 1,8-dibromooctane, TiCl4, acryloyl chloride and 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) monohydrate were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). 2,2′-Azoisobutyronitrile (AIBN), glucose oxidase (GOx, 100UmL−1, from Aspergillus niger), H2O2 (30wt.% in water), glucose, mannose, galactose, fructose, amino acids, ascorbic acid, dopamine and glutathione were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were of analytical grade (Tianjin, China). The water used throughout the experiments was supplied by Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). The stock solutions of TPEA (200μM), BAP (200μM based on AAPBA) and glucose (50mM) were prepared in water and stored at 4°C in the dark. The aqueous solution of GOx (20UmL−1) were prepared freshly before use.

Fluorescence spectra were measured by a Fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301pc, Japan) with Xenon lamp and 0.2-cm quartz cell at the slits of 10/10nm. Absorption spectra were measured on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1810, China) with 0.2-cm quartz cell. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectra were determined by 400MHz using a JEOL NMR instruments (Japan). The high resolution mass spectra (HRMS) were obtained from Bruker Daltonics APEX II 47e FT-ICR high resolution TOF MS system (America). Elemental analysis was performed on Elemental Analyzer (Vario EL Cube, Germany).

The zeta-potentials of BAP before and after addition of H2O2 were measured by dynamic light scattering (Nano ZS 3600, Malven, UK). H2O2 (50μL, 0 or 400μM) and BPA (50μL, 20μM) were added to PBS buffer (900μL, 10mM, pH 9.0). The obtained solutions were incubated at 25°C for 50min, and then their zeta-potentials were measured.
Fig. 2 illuminates the synthetic approach of BAP.

APBA monohydrate (0.77g, 5mmol) was dissolved in NaOH aqueous solution (10mL, 2M) and the obtained solution was cooled in an ice bath. Acryloyl chloride (1.05mL, 13mmol) was added drop-wise into the above solution under vigorously stirring. After 30min, the reaction mixture was stirred for an additional 2h at room temperature. Hydrochloric acid (2M) was then added to the mixture to adjust pH to 1.0. The precipitate was separated by filtration and washed with cold water. The product was redissolved in water (20mL) upon heating up to 80°C and yellow needle-shaped crystal of AAPBA was formed after incubation at 4°C overnight. The crystal was filtered and washed with cold water, and then dried under vacuum at 60°C. The yield of AAPBA was estimated to be 60% (0.57g). 1H NMR (400MHz, DMSO-d 6): δ 10.08 (s, 1H), 8.04 (s, 2H), 7.89 (s, 1H), 7.83 (d, 1H), 7.50 (d, 1H), 7.29 (t, 1H), 6.46 (dd, 1H), 6.25 (dd, 1H), 5.74 (dd, 1H). HRMS (ESI) Calcd. for [C9H10BNO3 +H+]: 192.0827, found: 192.0827.

AIBN (4.3mg, 0.026mmol) was added to the solution of AAPBA (100mg, 0.52mmol) and acrylamide (149mg, 2.1mmol) in ethanol (8mL). Then polymerization was performed at 65°C for 24h under nitrogen atmosphere. A white milky solution was obtained after polymerization and the solvent was evaporated under vacuum. The product was washed with acetone and then dried at room temperature. BAP was obtained as yellow powder in 68% yield (170mg). 1H NMR (400MHz, D2O): δ 7.75 (bm, 92H, ArH), 2.42 (bm, 103H, CH), 1.84 (bm, 200H, CH2).

TPEA (50μL, 20μM), BPA (50μL, 20μM) and GOx (50μL, 20UmL−1) were sequentially added to PBS buffer (800μL, 10mM, pH 9.0). Different concentrations of glucose (50μL) were added to the above solution, and the obtained solutions were incubated at 25°C for 50min. Fluorescence spectra were then recorded with the excitation and emission wavelengths at 326 and 472nm, respectively.

For human serum assay, 50μL trichloroacetic acid (10%, w/v) was added to 100μL serum sample to remove proteins. After centrifuged at 14,797 g for 10min, the obtained supernatant was diluted 10-fold with water. The treated serum sample was then assayed as described above.

The synthetic routes for TPEA and BAP are outlined in Fig. S1 and Fig. 2, respectively. TPEA with long hydrocarbon chain was synthesized due to that long hydrophobic arm facilitated the aggregation of positively charged tetraphenylethylene derivative on polyanion [22]. Acrylamide was used to promote the water-solubility of BAP [8]. The chemical structures of synthesized compounds were characterized with 1H NMR and HRMS (Fig. S2–S9). Elemental analysis (Table S1) and 1H NMR (Fig. S10) confirmed the successful synthesis of BAP. The mass percent of AAPBA was estimated to be 48% according to N elemental content. The molar percent of AAPBA calculated from 1H NMR was 23%, which was consistent with the result from elemental analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BAP-induced aggregation of TPEA for glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: To conduct the aforementioned strategy, this study first demonstrated that BAP was capable to induce the aggregation of TPEA. As shown in Fig. 3 A, fluorescence intensity increased gradually with adding BAP, which was the characteristic of TPEA aggregation. Dissociation of phenylboronic acid converted BAP into a polyanion that induced aggregation of TPEA via electrostatic interaction. Zeta-potential of BAP was measured and it was negative (−8.34±0.64mV), confirming that BAP possessed negative charges. Fluorescence intensity was almost constant when BAP concentration was above 1μM, and therefore 1μM BAP was used in further study.

Our purpose was to determine glucose via measurement of H2O2 produced in GOx-catalyzed glucose oxidation. Therefore, the response of TPEA-BAP to H2O2 was then investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, upon addition of H2O2 fluorescence intensity decreased. This was attributed to that H2O2 reacted with phenylboronic acid (p K a of 8.8) to generate a weaker acid phenol (p K a of 9.98) [14–16,25,26], which reduced negatively charges on BAP. Zeta-potential of BAP after addition of H2O2 was measured and it was increased to −1.47±0.22mV, verifying that the negative charges on BAP decreased.

Next, we investigated the response of TPEA-BAP to glucose in the presence of GOx (Fig. 3C). Fluorescence intensity was not decreased after addition of glucose or GOx. However, upon addition of both glucose and GOx fluorescence intensity decreased because of H2O2 production in enzymatic glucose oxidation. Fluorescence intensity increased slightly with adding GOx, which was attributed to GOx-induced aggregation of TPEA (Fig. S11). As pI of GOx was 4.0 [27], GOx carried negative charges in experimental condition (pH 7.4) and induced aggregation of TPEA via electrostatic interaction. As a consequence, GOx was always added in the reference solution to adjust the “zero” during the measurement.
To achieve optimal sensing performance, the experimental conditions including incubation time and pH were evaluated.

The effect of incubation time was investigated over a time period of 0–90min (Fig. 4 A). Fluorescence intensity decreased with time going on due to the oxidation of phenylboronic acid by H2O2 produced in enzymatic glucose oxidation, and was almost constant when incubation time was above 50min. As a sequence, 50min of incubation was employed in further study.

pH was another critical parameter influencing the sensing performance of TPEA-BAP. On one hand, there existed more negative charges on BAP in more basic conditions, benefiting the aggregation of TPEA (Fig. S12). On the other hand, higher pH values facilitated dissociation of H2O2 into the HO2 − anion that in turn reacted with phenylboronic acid [28]. The effect of pH was investigated by varying the pH between 4.5 and 9.0 (Fig. 4B). Further increase of pH was not tested since too strongly basic conditions were not user friendly, and may lead to hydrolysis of amide bonds on BAP as well [8]. It was observed that the response became stronger with increasing pH. Thus, pH 9.0 was employed in subsequent experiments.

We first investigated the influence of glucose analogues (fructose, galactose and mannose). As shown in Fig. 5 A, response of TPEA-BAP to these three sugars was neglectable and they had no effect on glucose detection. Since TPEA-BAP was designed to detect glucose in human serum, the effect of interfering substances existing in serum, including various amino acids, ascorbic acid, dopamine, glutathione, and uric acid, was also evaluated (Fig. 5B). It was observed that these substances almost had no effect on fluorescence intensity of TPEA-BAP and their existence did not affect glucose detection. The above results indicated that TPEA-BAP exhibited excellent selectivity, and this was attributed to the specific catalysis of GOx toward glucose oxidation.

As shown in Fig. 6 A, fluorescence intensity decreased as glucose concentration increased. There was a good linear relationship (R 2 =0.9939) between the decreased fluorescence intensity and glucose concentration in the range from 4 to 50μM with limit of detection (LOD) of 2.6μM (S/N=3) (Fig. 6B).

To investigate the practical application of TPEA-BAP, glucose detection in human serum was carried out. Glucose concentration (5.4±0.2mM) measured by this method was well in agreement with that (5.2±0.4mM) obtained from o-toluidine method (Supplementary Material), indicating the feasibility of TPEA-BAP for blood glucose detection.

Table 1 lists the comparison between TPEA-BAP and reported phenylboronic acid-based fluorescent glucose probes [15,16,29–34]. It was observed that this method had much lower LOD than the reported ones. Moreover, TPEA-BAP had different response mechanism from reported probes based on GOx [15,16]. The reported response mechanism relied on electronic difference between phenylboronic acid (electron deficient group) and phenol (electron rich group), and the response mechanism of TPEA-BAP relied on acidity difference between phenylboronic acid (p K a of 8.8) and phenol (p K a of 9.98). Therefore, this work provides a new perspective for designing phenylboronic acid-based glucose probes.

In this work, a fluorescent probe, based on acidity difference between phenylboronic acid and phenol, was reported for glucose detection. This work not only provides a sensitive and selective sensing platform for glucose, but also proposes a new concept for designing phenylboronic acid-based glucose probes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21375052 and 21575055).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.155.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Therapies for hyperglycaemia-induced diabetic complications: from animal models to clinical trials
3D nanoporous gold scaffold supported on graphene paper: freestanding and flexible electrode with high loading of ultrafine PtCo alloy nanoparticles for electrochemical glucose sensing
Phosphomolybdic acid functionalized graphene loading copper nanoparticles modified electrodes for non-enzymatic electrochemical sensing of glucose
Gold nanoparticles assembly on electrospun poly(vinyl alcohol)/poly(ethyleneimine)/glucose oxidase nanofibers for ultrasensitive electrochemical glucose biosensing
Carbon coated magnetite nanoparticles with improved water-dispersion and peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric sensing of glucose
1,4-Benzenediboronic-acid-induced aggregation of gold nanoparticles: application to hydrogen peroxide detection and biotin avidin-mediated immunoassay with naked-eye detection
Triple-enzyme mimetic activity of nickel-palladium hollow nanoparticles and their application in colorimetric biosensing of glucose
Glucose sensing via polyanion formation and induced pyrene excimer emission
Polyethyleneimine-capped silver nanoclusters as a fluorescence probe for sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
Development of dual-emission ratiometric probe-based on fluorescent silica nanoparticle and CdTe quantum dots for determination of glucose in beverages and human body fluids
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of sugars via a reversible disassembly and assembly of the peptide aggregates mediated by sugars
Nanoparticle-enhanced fluorescence emission for non-separation assays of carbohydrates using a boronic acid-alizarin complex
Boron-doped graphene quantum dots for selective glucose sensing based on the abnormal aggregation-induced photoluminescence enhancement
Boronic acid functionalized aza-bodipy (azaBDPBA) based fluorescence optodes for the analysis of glucose in whole blood
Fluorescence ratiometric assays of hydrogen peroxide and glucose in serum using conjugated polyelectrolytes
Highly specific-glucose fluorescence sensing based on boronic anthraquinone derivatives via the GOx enzymatic reaction
Fluorescent bioprobes: structural matching in the docking processes of aggregation-induced emission fluorogens on DNA surfaces
Nucleic acid-induced tetraphenylethene probe noncovalent self-assembly and the superquenching of aggregation-induced emission
Amino-modified tetraphenylethene derivatives as nucleic acid stain: relationship between the structure and sensitivity
Boronate affinity chromatography of cells and biomacromolecules using cryogel matrices

Enhanced binding capacity of boronate affinity adsorbent via surface modification of silica by combination of atom transfer radical polymerization and chain-end functionalization for high-efficiency enrichment of cis-diol molecules
The relationship among p K a, pH, and binding constants in the interactions between boronic acids and diols-it is not as simple as it appears
Substituent effects on the physical properties and p K a of phenol
Immobilization of glucose oxidase in alginate-chitosan microcapsules
Boronate oxidation as a bioorthogonal reaction approach for studying the chemistry of hydrogen peroxide in living systems
Fluorescent graphene quantum dots with a boronic acid appended bipyridinium salt to sense monosaccharides in aqueous solution
Fluorescence enhancement of cadmium selenide quantum dots assembled on silver nanoparticles and its application for glucose detection
Specific detection of D-glucose by a tetraphenylethene-based fluorescent sensor
Competitive analysis of saccharides or dopamine by boronic acid-functionalized CdSe-ZnS quantum dots
Boronic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive glucose determination in microdialysate
Surface modification and ratiometric fluorescence dual function enhancement for visual and fluorescent detection of glucose based on dual-emission quantum dots hybrid

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Huihui Li is a Ph.D candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University. Her main research interest is boronic acid-based biosensors.
Ling Zhu received her M.Sc. degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University in 2012. She works at Research Institute of Lanzhou Petrochemical Corporation currently.

Xinyue Zhu received her Ph.D degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University in 2016. She works at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University currently. Her main research interest is two-photo fluorescent biosensors.
Haixia Zhang is a professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University. Her main research interests are sample preparation and fluorescent biosensors.
